title: The Scholarship Kid
story:
I was a scholarship kid.

At Blair Academy, other boys had money. I waited tables in the dining hall. At Princeton, I worked every job I could find while writing my thesis on mutual funds.

Some people thought I didn't belong. Maybe they were right. But I had something they didn't—something to prove. That hunger carried me further than any advantage could have.

My senior thesis became my life's work. An academic paper about mutual funds turned into Vanguard. A scholarship kid who waited tables became the founder of the world's largest mutual fund company.

Here's what I learned: Your background doesn't define your future. Your work ethic does. Your persistence does. Your willingness to show up, every day, even when it's hard.

The people who succeed aren't always the smartest or the most connected. They're the ones who don't quit. Who keep going when others would give up. Who prove something to themselves.

You don't need to come from money. You don't need the right connections. You need discipline, persistence, and something to prove.

What are you proving? And to whom?
followup_questions: What are you trying to prove? And to whom?
What would the 10-years-from-now version of you want you to do?
Are you showing up, really showing up, every day?
